proof_stages.label: Euler patch and V-state equations
proof_stages.summary: The two-dimensional Euler equation is put in vorticity form, specialized to vortex patches, rewritten as a boundary singular-integral evolution, and then constrained to rigidly rotating V-states satisfying a time-independent boundary equation.

proof_stages.label: Bifurcation landscape and nonconvex target
proof_stages.summary: Known explicit disk and ellipse solutions, local and global bifurcation results, and numerical continuation pictures are reviewed to isolate the open nonperturbative problem of proving a nonconvex V-state, culminating in the stated sixfold theorem.

proof_stages.label: Polar reduction and Newton-type fixed point plan
proof_stages.summary: The boundary is written in polar coordinates to obtain a scalar nonlocal equation F(r)=0, and a numerically computed approximate solution r0 is corrected by solving the linearized equation plus a quadratic remainder via a contraction argument.

proof_stages.label: Linearization and symmetry removal
proof_stages.summary: The differential DF(r0) is expanded into multiplication and singular-integral pieces, dilation and rotation degeneracies are killed by working in an even/odd derivative space, and the unknown is transformed so the linear operator has the form I plus a compact operator.

proof_stages.label: Finite-rank computer-assisted invertibility
proof_stages.summary: Because a Neumann-series inversion is numerically impossible, the compact part is approximated by a finite-rank matrix, whose inverse and tail error are rigorously bounded to certify a quantitative inverse for the full linearized operator.

proof_stages.label: Defect and nonlinear estimates closing the contraction
proof_stages.summary: Quantitative bounds on the nonlinear remainder and on the defect F(r0), computed from explicit but complicated integrals, show that the fixed-point map sends a small L2 ball into itself and yields an H1 solution close to r0.

proof_stages.label: Certification of nonconvexity
proof_stages.summary: The rigorous closeness estimates are converted into a geometric inequality showing that one boundary point lies below the chord between neighboring vertices, hence convexity fails.

proof_stages.label: Regularity bootstrap to analyticity
proof_stages.summary: Starting from the H1 solution, repeated differentiation and positivity of the leading coefficient bootstrap the boundary to C-infinity, after which analytic regularity estimates upgrade the interface to analytic.
